project.name: stock-platform
project.description: Full-stack stock analysis platform with a React/Vite frontend, Express API, Interactive Brokers bridge, and Python/scikit-learn ML engine for OHLCV analysis and trade signals.

layers.name: Frontend UI
layers.description: React components, hooks, i18n, styling, and Vite entry files that make up the single-page stock analysis workspace.

layers.name: Backend API & Trading
layers.description: Express routes, API proxying, Interactive Brokers connectivity, order handling, and backend tests.

layers.name: Analysis Engine
layers.description: Python stock-data retrieval, moving-average feature engineering, RandomForest training, prediction, and analysis runtime dependencies.

layers.name: Project Knowledge
layers.description: Repository documentation and AGENTS guidance that explain setup, architecture, conventions, and operational risks.

layers.name: Configuration
layers.description: Package manifests, environment examples, linting, Vite, and Vitest configuration that shape local development and tests.

layers.name: CI Support
layers.description: Automation and test setup files that validate the frontend, backend, and Python analysis pieces in CI or local test runs.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand the platform as three cooperating runtimes: React/Vite in the browser, Express as the API and trading bridge, and Python for market data and ML recommendations. This gives the rest of the tour its shape before diving into implementation details.

tour.title: Frontend Boot
tour.description: The Vite HTML shell and React entry point show how the single-page app is mounted. The package and Vite configuration explain the development scripts, test environment, and React build setup behind that first render.
tour.languageLesson: Vite applications typically keep the HTML shell small and let the JavaScript entry point mount the React tree into a root element.

tour.title: Application State
tour.description: App.jsx is the frontend orchestration center: it tracks the selected instrument, fetches chart data, and coordinates the dialogs for orders, portfolio, screeners, and watchlists. The environment helper and persisted-state hook support that stateful workspace without introducing routing or global state libraries.

tour.title: Market Workspace
tour.description: The chart and search components are the main user workflow: find a symbol, fetch data, and inspect candlesticks, moving averages, volume, and AI signal state. Their CSS files show the visual layer that keeps the chart controls and autocomplete usable inside the dashboard.

tour.title: Trading Surfaces
tour.description: The trading, portfolio, orders, watchlist, and screener dialogs are the operational panels around the chart. Together they turn the analysis screen into a trading workspace by wiring frontend actions to backend portfolio, order, and symbol-selection flows.

tour.title: Backend Bridge
tour.description: The Express server is the integration hub for stock-history requests, Finnhub symbol search, Interactive Brokers portfolio/order state, and Python-service proxying. Its environment example and package manifest show which runtime settings and dependencies are required before real trading can work.

tour.title: Analysis Engine
tour.description: The Python analysis engine fetches OHLCV data, calculates moving-average and technical features, trains or loads a RandomForest classifier, and exposes both CLI and FastAPI behavior. The requirements file captures the scientific and web-service dependencies that make that engine run.
tour.languageLesson: This module mixes data-frame feature engineering with service boundaries, so stdout discipline matters: CLI calls must return pure JSON while diagnostics go to stderr.

tour.title: Localization Layer
tour.description: The i18n context, hook, and translation dictionary provide localized UI text to the React components without threading strings manually through every component. This is a small but important cross-cutting layer because nearly every dialog can depend on the same translation surface.

tour.title: Verification Path
tour.description: The frontend and backend test files show the expected behavior around UI orchestration and API validation. The GitHub Actions workflow ties those checks together so changes can be validated across the JavaScript and Python parts of the platform.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows use event triggers and jobs to codify the same commands developers run locally, making regression checks repeatable.
